data.title: SouthState signals 4% expense growth and mid- to upper single-digit loan expansion for 2026 as integration drives momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SouthState Bank Corporation (SSB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Corbett, CEO & Director, highlighted the successful completion and integration of the Independent Financial acquisition: "During this past year of 2025, the SouthState team successfully navigated through that initial period of high risks, the regulatory approvals and the systems conversions, and now we're on the other side, enjoying the rewards of a well-choreographed integration." Corbett also recognized Mark Thompson for leading the integration and announced his upcoming retirement.
  • Corbett stated that, excluding merger costs, "earnings per share in 2025 are up over 30%... double-digit growth in tangible book value per share... raising the dividend by 11% and share repurchases." He noted 8% loan and deposit growth during the quarter.
  • The company executed a share repurchase of 2 million shares in Q4 and the Board authorized a new plan for an additional 5.56 million shares. Corbett emphasized, "We didn't want to miss the opportunity to retire shares when there was such a disconnect between the fundamental performance of the bank and the valuation."
  • CFO William Matthews reported, "We had a good quarter to close out a very good year with PPNR of $323 million and $2.47 in EPS, resulting in a full year PPNR of $1.27 billion and EPS of $9.50. Our return on tangible common equity for the year was approximately 20%." Matthews also noted the efficiency ratio remained below 50% for both the quarter and the year, and capital ratios ended strong with a TCE ratio of 8.8% and CET1 at 11.4%.

Outlook

  • Margin guidance remains unchanged, with Stephen Young, Chief Strategy Officer, stating, "We would expect NIM to continue to be between 3.80% to 3.90% in 2026." Interest-earning assets are expected to average $61 billion to $62 billion, and loan accretion is forecasted at $125 million for 2026.
  • Corbett reiterated previous guidance for mid- to upper single-digit loan growth in 2026, supported by a $5 billion pipeline. He noted, "Our guidance previously for 2026 was mid- to upper single-digit loan growth. We still think that, that is appropriate as we see these pipelines build and hold."
  • Noninterest expense is projected to increase by 4% over 2025's $1.407 billion, reflecting investments in revenue producers and inflationary pressures, according to Matthews.

Financial Results

  • Matthews detailed, "Net interest income of $581 million, which was down $19 million from Q3 or up $14 million, excluding accretion." Loan and deposit growth both reached 8% annualized in Q4. Noninterest income rose to $106 million, driven by the Capital Markets division's $31 million revenue.
  • Cost of deposits and total cost of funds both declined, with deposit costs down 9 basis points and total cost of funds down 14 basis points. Loan yields of 6.13% were down 35 basis points, aligning with new loan origination coupons of 6.06%.
  • The company repurchased 2 million shares at an average price of $90.65. Total payout ratio was just shy of 100% for the quarter. Tangible book value per share grew 10% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • John McDonald, Truist Securities, asked about net interest margin assumptions; Young confirmed that guidance and deposit beta expectations are unchanged from last quarter. Corbett discussed loan growth momentum, citing a 16% quarter-over-quarter increase in production and a stable $5 billion pipeline.
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler, inquired about hiring activity; Corbett stated the bank had added 550 to 600 commercial RMs and may increase that by 10% to 15% in the next year or two. Scouten also asked about the sustainability of correspondent banking performance; Young explained that while recent performance benefited from rate changes, the business is expected to average $25 million per quarter in 2026.
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan, sought clarity on Q4 expense uptick; Matthews attributed it to performance-related compensation, seasonal factors, and increased business development spending, all incorporated into the 4% NIE growth guidance.
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW, asked about loan repricing dynamics; Young provided details on both legacy and acquired loan books, indicating a net positive repricing environment and a sustainable NIM range.
  • Jonathan Rau, Barclays, queried technology investments; Young described ongoing investments in commercial loan servicing, AI, and FX platforms, with a focus on revenue-producing talent.
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson, questioned loan production in Texas and Colorado; Matthews said combined Q4 production was $888 million, up 15% from Q3, with 17 of 26 new RMs in those markets.
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group, asked about banker recruitment efforts; Corbett described an active pipeline, with conversations growing from 200 to 237 bankers quarter-over-quarter.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral-to-positive tone, focusing on sustainability of growth, margin guidance, hiring, and expense control. Questions indicated confidence in management's ability to deliver on guidance, but also probed for details on drivers and sustainability.
  • Management presented a confident and constructive outlook, using phrases like "we're carrying that momentum into 2026" and "the fundamentals of the company are as good as they've ever been." Tone in Q&A remained steady and transparent, with detailed responses and reiteration of prior guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts' sentiment is stable, with continued focus on growth and integration outcomes, but with increased emphasis on hiring and leveraging market disruption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NIM and loan growth remains consistent, with management reiterating prior targets and maintaining a stable macro outlook.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from integration risk to execution on organic growth and revenue expansion, particularly in Texas and Colorado.
  • Analysts' questions evolved from integration and credit quality concerns in Q3 to hiring plans, expense management, and the sustainability of recent performance in Q4.
  • Key metrics such as EPS, tangible book value, and payout ratios showed improvement, while expense guidance and hiring initiatives became more prominent in management's remarks.
  • Management's confidence level is unchanged, but emphasis has shifted toward growth opportunities and capital deployment, supported by strong capital ratios and operational performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified performance-based compensation, seasonality, and the timing of hiring as drivers of higher fourth quarter expenses, but maintained that these are accounted for in 2026 guidance.
  • Questions were raised about the sustainability of noninterest income and correspondent banking performance, with management noting variability but projecting stable averages.
  • Substandard loan increases were attributed to a handful of multifamily properties in lease-up with strong equity positions, and credit concerns were downplayed.

Final Takeaway

SouthState Bank Corporation closes 2025 with integration risks largely behind it and a clear focus on organic growth, particularly in new markets. The company projects stable margins and mid- to upper single-digit loan growth for 2026 while expecting a 4% increase in noninterest expenses as it continues investing in revenue producers. Management maintains confidence in its strategic direction and capital strength, positioning the bank to leverage ongoing market disruption and drive further shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: enGene extends momentum with another 14% surge on Friday
data.description:
enGene Holdings Inc. (ENGN) climbed another 14% on Friday, adding to gains posted over the previous two sessions. Earlier in the week, the company announced an expanded debt facility, helping the stock extend an advance that has marked most of January so far.

Going into Friday's close, ENGN was trading at $12.12, an advance of $1.48 on the session. The rally of nearly 14% followed a similar 14% advance logged in the previous session. This followed an advance of 2.6% on Wednesday.

Overall, ENGN is on track to finish higher in eight of the past nine trading sessions. The stock has now climbed about 47% since its close on Jan. 6. Friday's surge also allowed the stock to set a new 52-week high of $12.25.

After the closing bell on Tuesday, the clinical-stage, non-viral genetic medicines company announced an expanded $125M debt facility with Hercules Capital, Inc. This included up to $100M in additional loan proceeds and $25M advanced to refinance its existing debt facility.

"This additional access to capital strengthens our balance sheet and provides us financial flexibility as we plan for a BLA filing for detalimogene in the second half of this year and potential commercial launch in 2027," CEO Ron Cooper said of the deal.

data.title: First Western signals continued NIM expansion and double-digit loan growth in 2026 amid market disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Western Financial, Inc. (MYFW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Scott Wylie, Founder, Chairman, CEO & President, reported "positive trends in many areas, including loan growth, net interest margin expansion, well-managed operating expenses and generally stable asset quality. This resulted in an increase in our level of profitability." He highlighted a conservative approach to loan production and described loan growth as "diversified across our markets, industries and loan types." Wylie also stated that tangible book value per share increased 1.6% in the quarter and noted a $0.10 per share after-tax reduction due to a write-down of an OREO property.
  • Julie Courkamp, COO & Director, stated loans held for investment increased $59 million from the prior quarter, with new loan production at $146 million for Q4. She emphasized, "The average rate on new production being 6.36% in this quarter."
  • Courkamp detailed deposit trends, reporting a $102 million increase in total deposits, with average deposits up 10% year-over-year. She noted outflows from title company operating accounts but highlighted successful new deposit relationships and the runoff of high-cost deposits.
  • David Weber, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "Our gross revenue increased 1.5% from the prior quarter, primarily due to an increase in net interest income. Relative to the fourth quarter of 2024, our gross revenue increased 12.2%." He added, "Our net interest income increased 5.6% from the prior quarter and 21.7% from the fourth quarter of 2024 due to an increase in our net interest margin." Weber also noted a 17 basis point improvement in NIM to 2.71%.

Outlook

  • Wylie projected, "Our loan deposit pipelines remain strong and should continue to result in solid balance sheet growth in 2026 with loan and deposit growth at similar levels to what we had in 2025." He indicated ongoing market disruption is "creating opportunities for us to add banking talent, and we will continue to take advantage of these opportunities if and when they materialize."
  • The company expects "positive trends in our net interest margin, our fee income and more operating leverage resulting from our disciplined expense control." Wylie cautioned, "We had net interest margin of 26 basis points in 2025. And while we expect further expansion in 2026, it may not be at the same level as we saw last year."
  • Management is not expecting "any meaningful deterioration in asset quality" and aims for "steady improvement in our financial performance and further value being created for our shareholders in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Reported net income was $3.3 million or $0.34 per diluted share for Q4, compared to $3.2 million or $0.32 per diluted share in Q3.
  • Noninterest income decreased by approximately $800,000 from the prior quarter due to seasonal declines in mortgage sales and lower risk management and insurance fees.
  • Operating expenses increased by $1.2 million, primarily due to a $1.4 million one-time write-down of an OREO property, while other noninterest expense categories remained consistent or decreased slightly.
  • Asset quality remained stable, with decreases in nonaccrual loans and NPAs, and the last piece of OREO is under contract for sale.

Q&A

  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: Asked about NIM outlook and loan repricing. Weber responded, "We do expect further expansion to continue through 2026. Now it may not be at the same level that we saw through 2025 ... but we do expect to continue through 2026." Weber added, "We have about $250 million in fixed loans ... maturing over the next year. And those average yield on those is in the low 5s."
  • Rabatin inquired about asset/wealth management and mortgage. Wylie explained, "A lot of the lower-yielding categories and the fixed rate categories have had reductions. We're actually in the higher-yielding categories for us on the AUM side that we're seeing improvements." Courkamp reported, "Q4 mortgage production, Q1 mortgage production for us is typically lower just given the seasonality. But we continue to remain very focused as a strategic part of our business."
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about expense growth and profitability targets. Wylie said, "We wanted to keep our expense below $20 million a quarter ... if we see opportunities ... we want to look at it and support you with that." On ROA, Wylie said, "We have said our near-term objective here is to get to a 1% ROA, which would be 3.50-ish."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about deposit beta and NIM pressure. Weber stated, "Our NIM in the month of December was 2.72%." On deposit beta, Weber said, "I think we can hold that."
  • William Dezellem, Tieton Capital: Asked about mortgage loans for sale and construction/development loans. Weber explained the mortgage held-for-sale balance was "timing dynamics." Wylie said the reduction in construction loans was "much more the former than the latter"—an intentional strategy.
  • Ross Haberman, RLH Investments: Asked about mortgage market outlook and possible acquisitions. Wylie and Courkamp described ongoing investment in mortgage loan officer headcount and a focus on organic growth, not acquisitions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were probing about NIM sustainability, expense discipline, asset quality, and loan growth, with a neutral to slightly positive undertone as management provided direct responses and detail on strategic initiatives.
  • Management's tone was confident, with Wylie stating, "The positive trends we're seeing in a number of key areas are expected to continue, which we believe will result in a steady improvement in our financial performance and further value being created for our shareholders in 2026." During Q&A, management remained constructive, directly addressing concerns and providing operational details.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment was more optimistic, with a stronger focus on offensive growth strategies and leveraging market disruption for talent and client acquisition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan growth accelerated, with loans held for investment increasing $59 million in Q4 vs. $50 million in Q3; new loan production remained steady at $146 million both quarters.
  • Deposit growth slowed to $102 million in Q4 from $320 million in Q3, attributed to seasonal outflows, but underlying new deposit relationships remained strong.
  • Net interest margin increased 17 basis points to 2.71% in Q4 from a Q3 decline to 2.54%.
  • Operating expenses rose in Q4 due to a one-time OREO write-down, while core expenses remained flat. Noninterest income declined sequentially, reversing the Q3 increase.
  • Management's tone shifted more decisively toward growth, with a greater emphasis on market disruption opportunities and expansion into Arizona.
  • Analysts shifted from focusing on deposit stickiness and balance sheet deployment in Q3 to NIM sustainability, expense management, and asset quality in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Market competition for loans and deposits remains intense, with management emphasizing a conservative approach to underwriting and pricing.
  • Seasonal and structural declines in noninterest income raise questions about fee income growth, though management expects improvement due to leadership changes.
  • The $1.4 million OREO write-down was a one-time item, and management indicated the property is under contract for sale.
  • Construction and development lending has been intentionally reduced as a risk mitigation strategy.
  • Management noted ongoing monitoring of asset quality and the potential impact of economic shifts, but reported no signs of deterioration.

Final Takeaway

First Western Financial delivered improved profitability in the fourth quarter, driven by loan growth, disciplined expense management, and net interest margin expansion. Management expects to maintain this positive momentum into 2026, targeting continued balance sheet growth and further NIM improvement, while leveraging market disruption to attract talent and clients. The company remains focused on organic growth, maintaining asset quality, and achieving greater operating leverage, with a clear objective of reaching a 1% return on assets as key initiatives mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Netflix snaps six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) shares snapped six straight sessions of losses, as the stock closed over 3% higher at $86.19 on Friday.

The streaming giant lost about 6% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock rose over 5% last year, underperforming the nearly 17% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

NFLX is down nearly 9% over the past one month.

Netflix began its downward trend from Jan. 14, 2026. The stock closed 2% lower on Thursday at $83.54. Until now, January has not been a favorable month for the company, as the stock has just closed in green thrice, compared to the 11 sessions in red.

Netflix was in focus this week after posting fourth-quarter results that narrowly beat expectations. However, shares tumbled as the company’s bidding war with Paramount (PKSY) to take over Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) took the limelight.

Analysts, in general, found Netflix’s quarterly results somewhat “mixed” and “underwhelming.” However, they said that the streaming giant is poised for long-term growth and that the current weakness is 'a buying opportunity' for investors.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, NFLX has a Hold rating with a score of 3.03 out of 5. The company received an A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it got a D- in the valuation factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 28 analysts gave NFLX a Buy and above, 14 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and two recommended Strong Sell. Seeking Alpha analysts are also cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

Earlier this week, Netflix made a revised all-cash offer for Warner Bros. Discovery at $27.75 per share. 

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said that Netflix could be forced to match Paramount’s $30/share offer if it wants to win the bid.

“In my view, this could increase bridge financing and pressure the 2026 margin expectations. A downgrade from the 31.5% operating margin could hurt the stock,” it added.

data.title: Amerant outlines 7%–9% loan growth for 2026 amid balance sheet optimization efforts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amerant Bancorp Inc. (AMTB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Interim CEO Carlos Iafigliola stated the Board approved a new 3-year strategic plan in December, emphasizing a sequenced road map to stabilize, optimize, and grow the organization. He explained, “Our plan is built on a disciplined and sequenced road map in order to stabilize, optimize and a disciplined and sequence road map and grow the organization.” Immediate priorities include credit transformation, balance sheet optimization, and operational efficiency.
  • Iafigliola detailed efforts to restore predictability to the loan portfolio’s credit performance, targeting exits from non-core markets and large exposures while avoiding migration into criticized buckets. He described an intentional reduction of non-core funding and asset optimization below the $10 billion threshold.
  • The company has initiated an AI project focused on optimizing processes and launched a share repurchase program. Iafigliola also commented on Venezuela, noting “almost $2 billion in deposits, significant AUM and close to 50,000 customers in this country,” and highlighted renewed opportunities due to changes in U.S. oil extraction policy.
  • CFO Sharymar Yepez reported, “Total assets were $9.8 billion as of the end of the fourth quarter, a decrease from $10.4 billion as of the end of the third quarter.” She attributed the decrease to reduction in wholesale funding, sale of investments, and efforts to reduce higher cost deposits. Yepez added, “Diluted income per share for the fourth quarter was $0.07 compared to $0.35 in the third quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management projects loan balances for Q1 2026 to remain at Q4 2025 levels, with full-year 2026 loan growth estimated between 7% to 9%, primarily driven by funding of existing lines. Projected deposit growth is expected to match loan growth. Net interest margin guidance is in the 3.65% to 3.70% range. Expenses are expected to be $70 million to $71 million in the first half of 2026, declining to $67 million to $68 million by year end.
  • Iafigliola emphasized, “Return on asset is definitely one that we really want to accomplish by year-end, getting as close as possible to the 1%.” The efficiency ratio target is “getting as closer as possible to the 60% at year-end.”

Financial Results

  • Total assets decreased to $9.8 billion from $10.4 billion in the previous quarter. Cash and cash equivalents ended at $470.2 million, down from $630.9 million. Total investments fell to $2.1 billion from $2.3 billion. Total gross loans dropped by $244.6 million to $6.7 billion. Total deposits were $7.8 billion, down $514 million from $8.3 billion. Broker deposits declined to $435.7 million from $550.2 million.
  • Assets under management increased by $87.2 million to $3.3 billion. Net interest income was $90.2 million, down from $94.2 million. Noninterest income was $22 million, up from $17.3 million, aided by a $3.3 million gain on the sale and leaseback of two banking centers. Noninterest expense rose to $106.8 million due to loan valuation expenses, contract termination costs, staff separation, and impairment charges. Core noninterest expense was $77.6 million. The efficiency ratio was 95.19%, impacted by decreased net income and higher expenses. Pre-provision net revenue was $5.4 million, while core PPNR was $29.3 million. CET1 rose to 11.8% from 11.54%, supported by share repurchases and dividends.
  • Nonperforming assets increased to $187 million or 1.9% of total assets from $140 million or 1.3%. Provision for credit losses was $3.5 million, down from $14.6 million. Gross charge-offs totaled $29.5 million, partially offset by $11.1 million in recoveries. Allowance for credit losses coverage ratio decreased to 1.20% from 1.37%.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked which metrics should be used to measure the 3-year strategic program’s success. Iafigliola responded that improvements in credit quality metrics and a disciplined loan origination approach are critical. Yepez added that operational efficiency, specifically the efficiency ratio and ROA, will also be key measures.
  • Rose followed up on financial targets for years two and three. Iafigliola said, “Return on asset is definitely one that we really want to accomplish by year-end, getting as close as possible to the 1%,” and the efficiency ratio goal is “getting as closer as possible to the 60% at year-end.”
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Asked about drivers for expense reduction and how to view normalized expenses. Yepez attributed reductions to optimizing higher cost deposits, marketing spend, and strategic hiring. She guided for $67–$68 million for Q4 2026 normalized expenses. Iafigliola added that marketing savings alone would total more than $6 million in 2026.
  • Gunther inquired about asset quality trends. Yepez said, “We do project an improvement in the criticized portfolio,” with provision guidance in the 40 to 45 basis points range.
  • Gunther asked about international deposit trends and Venezuela. Iafigliola said it was “too soon, but we see very good signs and very fast progression,” expecting a positive impact once oil production surges. Yepez noted a Q4 increase in personal international balances, offset by commercial reductions.
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Queried if deposit runoff was intentional and the impact of being below $10 billion. Iafigliola confirmed the runoff was deliberate to optimize the balance sheet and supported staying below $10 billion unless core growth justifies it. Lay also asked about brokered deposits and restructuring charges, with Iafigliola stating the intent is to keep brokered deposits low and non-GAAP adjustments minimized for a cleaner 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on execution, clarity around targets, and the credibility of improvement efforts, with a neutral to slightly negative tone reflected in repeated requests for specific metrics and milestones.
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, frequently referencing discipline, alignment, and aspirational targets. Phrases like “we are aligned, we are committed, and we are executing with discipline” and “we are confident in both the durability of our franchise and the opportunities ahead” signaled steady conviction.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed more focus on operational execution and accountability, with management more forthcoming about specific targets and expense management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a new 3-year strategic plan with explicit focus on credit quality, operational efficiency, and balance sheet optimization, replacing the previous quarter’s broader references to asset quality and expense reductions.
  • Asset levels and loan portfolios saw sharper reductions, while nonperforming assets and charge-offs rose. Noninterest expense spiked due to multiple one-time items, compared to more modest increases previously.
  • Guidance language evolved, with more detailed goals for ROA (1%), efficiency ratio (60%), expense levels, and loan growth (7%–9%) for the coming year. Analyst questions focused more tightly on metrics, targets, and normalization of costs.
  • Management’s tone shifted from general confidence to a more disciplined, target-driven posture, explicitly addressing past guidance misses and the need for measurable improvement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit quality remains a central risk, with increases in nonperforming assets and classified loans, though management has intensified review and risk identification measures.
  • Expense management poses a challenge, with significant non-core costs in Q4, but actions have been initiated to rationalize marketing and reduce recurring costs.
  • Exposure to Venezuela introduces both opportunity and potential volatility, as management monitors macroeconomic and policy developments.
  • Analyst concerns included the pace of asset quality improvement, the sustainability of cost reductions, and the credibility of long-term targets amid historical guidance shortfalls.

Final Takeaway

Amerant Bancorp’s management reaffirmed its strategic commitment to credit quality, operational efficiency, and disciplined growth, underpinned by a new 3-year plan and explicit targets for ROA, efficiency ratio, and loan growth. Although the quarter was marked by higher nonperforming assets and expenses, the leadership emphasized decisive actions and clear metrics for success, with a focus on stabilizing the business, optimizing the balance sheet, and capturing new opportunities, particularly in international markets. The company aims to build credibility with investors through transparent progress on cost reduction and asset quality while executing a measured path toward sustained performance improvements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Falcon's Beyond Global bounces back after recent slide; up 15% on Friday
data.description:
Falcon's Beyond Global, Inc. (FBYD) rallied more than 15% on Friday, bouncing back after a long string of losing sessions. 

Late in Friday's session, FBYD was up 96 cents to $6.96. The stock had been down 11 consecutive sessions going into Friday's trading and had finished lower in 15 of the previous 16 sessions. Even with Friday's rebound, the stock has fallen 59% since its close on Dec. 29.

Late last year, the company warned of liquidity issues, stating in its 10-Q that "substantial doubt exists as to the company’s ability to continue as a going concern."

"The company doesn't seem particularly well-managed financially," Seeking Alpha analyst Bashar Issa said in a deep dive published earlier this month. "A good product without financial discipline often yields disappointing investment results."

Still, Issa noted that "FBYD's Creative Group may surprise as Qiddiya parks open and deliveries finalize." He concluded: "I think FBYD is worth watching, but I wouldn't be rushing to buy the ticker anytime soon."

data.title: AI inference startup Baseten confirms $300M in new funding at $5B valuation
data.description:
Baseten, a startup focused on providing inference for artificial intelligence applications, said on Friday that it has raised $300M in a Series E funding round, confirming previous reports.

The new funding round, which includes Nvidia (NVDA), Battery Ventures, Blackbird Ventures, and others as investors, values the AI startup at $5B. 

“The last few years have been exhilarating: last year alone inference volume grew 100x,” Baseten wrote on its website. “We’ve had the privilege of partnering with some of the fastest-growing, highest-quality companies in the space—Abridge, Clay, Cursor, OpenEvidence, Mercor, Notion, and others—and a front-row seat to the real value they’re delivering to users. Looking ahead, demand for inference will only accelerate. Thinking and reasoning models require orders of magnitude more compute, and AI adoption across consumers and enterprises continues to compound. As AI becomes embedded in every product and workflow, inference isn’t just growing—it’s becoming one of the largest markets ever created. We’re building the software that powers it.”

The $300M Series E funding round is the company's third capital raise in 12 months.

Nebius (NBIS) recently partnered with Baseten to offer AI companies and enterprises text-to-video inferencing across all its cloud regions in the U.S., Finland, and France.

data.title: Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon's annual pay jumps 21% to $47M
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) Chairman and CEO David Solomon's total 2025 compensation rose to $47M from  $39M a year earlier, a 21% Y/Y jump, the investment bank disclosed on Friday.

He received a $2M base salary along with a $45M bonus in the form of stock, cash and carried interest, according to a U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filing.

In making its determination, the compensation committee considered a slew of positive developments, from significant shareholder value (+57% total shareholder return, +33% in quarterly dividend, and +6.2% book value per share) to strong financial performance (+27% in diluted EPS and a 230-basis-point improvement in return on average common equity).

By comparison, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon earned $43M in 2025, up 10.2% Y/Y.

data.title: Intel 10-K highlights external foundry revenue, potential upside for AMD: Wells Fargo
data.description:
After Intel (INTC) filed its annual 10-K report with its fourth-quarter results, some notable findings include the semiconductor giant's external foundry revenue and possible benefits for AMD (AMD), Wells Fargo said.

External foundry revenue for the full-year was $307M, including $222M in the fourth-quarter, up sharply from the $159M in 2024, Wells Fargo analyst Aaron Rakers wrote in a note to clients. However, the de-consolidation of Altera, which Intel partially sold to private equity firm Silver Lake in September 2025, contributed to fourth-quarter external revenue, Rakers added.

Intel generated $4.51B in foundry-related revenue for the period ending Dec. 27, out of a total $13.67B.

Rakers has an Equal-Weight rating and $45 price target on Intel.

Additionally, Rakers said the 10-K disclosed that 2025 server CPU volume grew 9% year-over-year. However, the average selling price fell 4%, due in part to pricing actions taken in the first-half of the year and a higher mix of “lower core count products,” Rakers added. As such, he believes that fourth-quarter shipments of server CPUs grew 8% year-over-year, down from 13% growth in the second-quarter and flat in the third quarter.

“While we have not yet seen preliminary 4Q25 server ship estimates, we note that Micron's (MU) outlook that CY25 server ship would increase ~15% y/y (reflecting a strong acceleration [through] 2HCY25; Gartner estimates 1Q25-3Q25 was +1% y/y) would imply 4Q25 server shipments up 15%-17% y/y,” Rakers said. “Our estimate of Intel's Xeon CPU ship at +8% y/y would leave us incrementally positive on AMD's server ship results for 4Q25.”

Intel shares tumbled more than 17% on Friday, while AMD shares rose more than 2%.

data.title: Life360 rallies more than 20% on preliminary results
data.description:
Life360, Inc. (LIF) jumped more than 20% on Friday following the company's announcement that it will report a strong operational performance for Q4 2025. The company revealed preliminary results that included Q4 Monthly Active Users of 95.8M, with the highest Q4 MAU additions in the firm's history.

The company added that full-year 2025 net additions reached 16.2 million, representing 20% growth YoY. For Q4, U.S. MAU rose 16% from last year, while international growth hit 26%.

The stock was higher by $12.25 in late Friday trading, climbing to $67.06. This represented a rally of more than 22%.

LIF had been drifting lower since October, coming off of a 52-week high of $112.54. The stock currently has a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of 3.07, representing a Hold, with an A grade for Growth but a D- for valuation.
